revision.text:
'''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[Nagano City]] regions in [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|12=draft|12=draft}} 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[Ninna-ji]]. In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[Hōjō Akitoki]].
In {{ill|Kenmu|ja|建武|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the {{ill|Jōwa|ja|貞和|12=draft|12=draft}} era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)

}}

revision.comment: 60 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:四宮荘 up to rev 102658109
revision.text:
'''四宮荘'''（しのみやのしょう）は、[[信濃国]][[更級郡]]四宮地区（現在の[[長野県]][[長野市]][[篠ノ井]]地区から[[千曲市]][[更埴]]地区）にあった[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]。[[千曲川]]を渡る[[津]]があったとされる。
== 概要 == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|武水別神社]] 荘名は信濃国四宮の[[武水別神社]]の鎮座に由来し同社神官家四宮氏の社里であったことからとの説がある<ref name="#1">「『角川日本地名大辞典」p.568</ref>。北は聖川を境に石河荘（仁和寺領）、南は小谷荘（石清水八幡領）に接する<ref>「長野県の地名」平凡社 p.833</ref>。『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]2年[[3月12日 (旧暦)|3月12日]]（[[1186年]][[4月3日]]）条に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]から[[源頼朝]]に示された「関東御知行国々内乃具未済庄々注文」では荘内が北条、南条に分けられ、ともに[[仁和寺]]領。『吾妻鏡』同4年（[[1188年]]）[[2月2日 (旧暦)|2月2日]]条には、篠宮神社とあり地頭が年貢や領家得文を未納し、仁和寺が[[鎌倉幕府]]に訴えている。のちに仁和寺北院領として証性上人、証恵上人が安堵している。[[建治]]2年（1275年）当時から四宮左衛門跡の一族が[[御家人]]として存続。また[[勅旨田]]があり、[[永仁]]6年（[[1298年]]）8月2日の仁和寺宮令旨では、「北院領四宮勅旨」を[[高山寺|高山寺領]]として安堵しており、[[延慶 (日本)|延慶]]3年（[[1310年]]）6月16日、[[伏見天皇|伏見上皇]]は院宣によって安堵している。鎌倉時代末期には[[北条顕時]]が地頭を務めていた。
[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（1335年）の[[中先代の乱]]では[[諏訪神党]]の[[保科氏]]、関屋氏（[[皆神山]][[出早雄命|出速雄神社]]神官家を世襲）が武水別神社神官家の四宮左衛門太郎（四宮荘）と共に船山守護所（千曲市小船山）を襲撃（[[青沼合戦]]）し、北条残党として[[建武の新政|建武政権]]に反乱を起こして[[小笠原貞宗]]に鎮圧された<ref>長野県史 通史編 第3巻 p.159</ref>。これにより保科氏は高遠に落ち延び、隆盛時には[[地頭]]と[[郡司]]をも兼ねたと云われる四宮氏は社里を焼かれ滅びたと伝わる。同年11月9日には[[足利尊氏]]により荘内北条地頭職が[[諏訪円忠]]（頼貞）に渡付され、[[天龍寺]]に寄進された。[[貞和]]2年（1346）の諏訪円忠四宮荘在家注進状によれば、荘内北条は在家35宇のうち、定在家が25宇が、免在家が10宇、定田41町9段のうち、免田が28町5段で、100貫文を領家仁和寺に、300貫文を天龍寺に年貢が割り当てられている<ref>長野県史 通史編 第3巻 p.351</ref>。北条は[[長谷寺 (長野市)|長谷寺]]の参道以北、南条は佐野川以北の[[稲荷山宿|稲荷山]]、[[桑原宿|桑原]]方面と推定される<ref name="#1" />。守護小笠原貞宗の命で[[赤沢氏]]（常興）が地頭となる。[[大塔合戦]]（応永7年1400年）の際は[[塩崎城]]に籠ったため戦場にもなった。敗戦の[[小笠原長秀]]が[[大井氏]]の仲介を得て逃亡後は[[村上氏|村上満信]]の支配下で赤沢氏は残留。しかし[[永享の乱]]（永享10年1438年）に乗じた守護[[小笠原政康]]は[[村上氏|村上頼清]]を排して入り南条には赤沢対馬守（後に桑原対馬と呼ばれる）を入れ[[小坂城]]を築かせる。桑原氏は[[漆田原の戦い]]（文安3年1446年）等の小笠原宗家相続の騒乱に乗じて赤沢氏を圧迫して塩崎城に入り[[塩崎氏]]を名乗る。[[武田氏]]侵攻の頃は[[村上義清]]の支配下にあったが天文22年（1553年）塩崎氏は[[千曲川]]対岸の[[屋代氏]]、[[雨宮氏]]らと共に[[真田幸隆]]の調略に応じ武田氏に臣従。天正12年（1584年）[[上杉景勝]]が稲荷山に築城の際松田氏に八幡神領（[[小谷荘]]）の管理を命じている。
[[嘉暦]]4年（1329年）には[[諏訪大社]]の造営を負担し、以後複数回にわたって上社の神役を勤仕し（『諏訪御符礼之古書』）、[[天正]]6年（[[1578年]]）の造営清書帳では「四宮之郷」と記されている。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 <small>長野県</small>』（角川書店、1990年） *『[[長野県史]] 通史編 第2巻 中世1』 *『長野県史 通史編 第3巻 中世2』
{{デフォルトソート:しのみやのしよう}} [[Category:信濃国の荘園]] [[Category:長野県域の荘園]] [[Category:長野市の歴史]] [[Category:千曲市の歴史]]

'''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[Nagano City]] regions in [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[Ninna-ji]]. In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[Hōjō Akitoki]].
In {{ill|Kenmu|ja|建武|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the {{ill|Jōwa|ja|貞和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shinomiya no Sho}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinomiya Manor}}

The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:長野県域の荘園
revision.text:
'''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[Nagano City]] regions in [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|qq=}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)

'''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[Nagano City]] regions in [[Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|qq=}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)
{{translated page|ja|四宮荘|version=102658109|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}
[[sn:Shinomiya Manor]] [[ja:四宮荘]]
<!--Shinomiya Manor--> [[Category:信濃国の荘園]] [[Category:History of Chikuma]] [[Category:History of Nagano]] [[Category:長野県域の荘園]]

In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

'''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|qq=}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].

Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|qq=|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Translated page but missing wikidata]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|qq=|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)
{{translated page|ja|四宮荘|version=102658109|comment=Imported full ja history}}

By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|qq=|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)

In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)
{{translated page|ja|四宮荘|version=102658109|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)
{{translated page|ja|四宮荘|version=102658109|comment=Imported full ja history}}

In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 20 Nagano'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1990) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-2 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 2, Medieval History 1) * ''Nagano-ken-shi Tsūshi-hen Dai-3 Kan'' (Nagano Prefectural History Vol. 3, Medieval History 2)

Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', in the entry for [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of [[:en:Ninna-ji|Ninna-ji]]. In [[:en:Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]].
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of [[:en:Hōjō Akitoki|Hōjō Akitoki]].
In [[:en:Jianwu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's [[:en:Hoshina clan|Hoshina clan]] and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by [[:en:Ogasawara Sadamune|Ogasawara Sadamune]]. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. A document from the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa]] era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
== References == <references/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四宮荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}'', in the entry for {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of {{ill|Ninna-ji|en|Ninna-ji|WD=Q1202871|lt=Ninna-ji}}. In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}.
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of {{ill|Hōjō Akitoki|en|Hōjō Akitoki|WD=Q11402208|lt=Hōjō Akitoki}}.
In {{ill|Jianwu|en|Jianwu|WD=Q11065423|lt=Kenmu}} 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan}} and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma City}}, Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|en|Ogasawara Sadamune|WD=Q7079835|lt=Ogasawara Sadamune}}. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. A document from the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa}} era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of {{ill|Hōjō Akitoki|en|Hōjō Akitoki|WD=Q11402208|lt=Hōjō Akitoki|1=Hōjō Akitoki}}.
In {{ill|Jianwu|en|Jianwu|WD=Q11065423|lt=Kenmu|1=Jianwu}} 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma City|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|en|Ogasawara Sadamune|WD=Q7079835|lt=Ogasawara Sadamune|1=Ogasawara Sadamune}}. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}. A document from the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>

'''Shinomiya Sho''' (四宮荘) was an [[estate (Japan)|estate]] (荘園) located in the Shinomiya area of {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, covering what is now the [[Chikuma City|Chikuma]] and [[:en:Nagano City|Nagano City]] regions in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}. It is believed to have included a crossing over the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]].
== Overview == [[File:Takemizuwake-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|Takemizuwake Shrine]] The estate's name is derived from the [[Takemizuwake Shrine]] (武水別神社), which was situated within the estate and served as the shrine of the local Shinomiya clan, who acted as shrine officials.<ref name="#1">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten," p. 568</ref> To the north, it bordered the Ishikawa Sho (仁和寺領), marked by the Hijiri River, and to the south, the Kodani Sho (石清水八幡領).<ref>"Nagano-ken no Chimei," Heibonsha, p. 833</ref>
According to the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}'', in the entry for {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji|1=Bunji (era)}} 2, 3rd month, 12th day (April 3, 1186), the estate was split into two parts, Hokujō (north) and Nanjō (south), both under the control of {{ill|Ninna-ji|en|Ninna-ji|WD=Q1202871|lt=Ninna-ji|1=Ninna-ji}}. In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji|1=Bunji (era)}} 4 (1188), the estate's steward failed to pay annual dues, leading Ninna-ji to petition the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}.
By the end of the Kamakura period, the Shinomiya clan and their branches continued to exist as retainers of the shogunate. Records show that in {{ill|Enkei|ja|延慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkei|1=Enkei (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1306063}} 3 (1310), the estate was reaffirmed to Ninna-ji's North Monastery (Kitain) through imperial orders.<ref>''Nagano Ken Monjo,'' Volume 3, p. 159</ref> By the late period, it came under the stewardship of {{ill|Hōjō Akitoki|en|Hōjō Akitoki|WD=Q11402208|lt=Hōjō Akitoki|1=Hōjō Akitoki}}.
In {{ill|Jianwu|en|Jianwu|WD=Q11065423|lt=Kenmu|1=Jianwu}} 2 (1335), during the {{ill|Nakasendai Rebellion|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasendai Rebellion|qq=}}, the [[Shinomiya clan]] joined forces with the Suwa Faith's {{ill|Hoshina clan|en|Hoshina clan|WD=Q2661264|lt=Hoshina clan|1=Hoshina clan}} and the Sekiya family in attacking the military post at Funayama (present-day {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma City|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, Komune), which was ultimately suppressed by {{ill|Ogasawara Sadamune|en|Ogasawara Sadamune|WD=Q7079835|lt=Ogasawara Sadamune|1=Ogasawara Sadamune}}. This led to the decline of the Shinomiya clan, with records stating their holdings and shrine were destroyed.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
The estate then transferred to [[Tenryū-ji]] by the efforts of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}. A document from the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era (1346) detailed a division of land tax revenues between the resident temples and the Tenryū-ji.<ref>"Nagano Ken Monjo," Volume 3, p. 351</ref>
== Significant Events == In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 6 (1578), the estate played a role in maintaining the [[Suwa Shrine]]. Records indicate that Shinomiya continued to fulfill its obligations for shrine construction and rituals into the late medieval period.<ref>"Suwa Gofurei no Kosho," p. 351</ref>
